perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means nonpublic information that a party to this Agreement (“Disclosing Party”) designates as being confidential to the partythat receives such information (“Receiving Party”) or which, under thecircumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential by theReceiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means any information, whether public or nonpublic, that a party to this Agreement (“Disclosing Party”) designates as being confidential to the partythat receives such information (“Receiving Party”) or which, under thecircumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential by theReceiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: By including 'public' information within the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the contract contradicts established legal norms that protect only non-public or proprietary information under trade secret or confidentiality laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South Africa's Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 and common law principles regarding trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.law_citation: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013, Section 1
perturbation.law_explanation: The Act defines personal and confidential information with an emphasis on its private and non-public nature. Including publicly available information undermines the purpose of the Act and trade secret laws, creating ambiguity about what is truly protected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Page Not Found | GovInfo Skip to main content Page Not Found Help Error occurred. The page you requested cannot be found. Please report this error to askGPO. Provide the following information to help us resolve this problem: the URL of the page you were trying to access, the steps you followed to produce the error, specific search or browse terms, and/or a screenshot of the page where the error occurred. Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: event: Privacy PIE with EFF's Andrew Crocker From the Organizers:We're excited to welcome Andrew Crocker, Surveillance Litigation Director for the EFF, as this month's EFA Edition of Privacy PIE! As Surveillance Litigation... EFF has filed an amicus brief in Trabajadores v. Bessent , a case concerning the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) sharing protected personal tax information with the Department of Homeland Security for the purposes of immigration enforcement. Our expertise in privacy and data sharing makes us the ideal organization to step in... For the first time, EFF is excited to be at The Diana Initiative ! Be sure to stop by our info table at the conference to learn more about our latest work defending digital freedom for all. We're going to be handing out stickers and spreading the word about privacy... EFF is excited to support SeaGL (the Seattle GNU/Linux Conference )! Taking place at the University of Washington, SeaGL is your one-stop-shop to learn about free and open source software and hardware. SeaGL is free to attend , with no registration required. Just show up and enjoy the community! More about ... The State of Washington enacted one of the strongest consumer data privacy laws in recent years: the “My Health, My Data” Act. While the law only applies to one category of personal data—our health information—its structure could be used to protect all manner of data. Keeping up on the latest digital rights news has never been easier. With a new look, EFF's EFFector newsletter covers the latest details on our work defending your rights to privacy and free expression online. EFFector 37.7 covers some of the very sneaky tactics that Meta has been using... The cryptography that protects our privacy and security online relies on the fact that even the strongest computers will take essentially forever to do certain tasks, like factoring prime numbers and finding discrete logarithms which are important for RSA encryption , Diffie-Hellman key ... perturbation.justification: The changed text includes 'public' information within the definition of 'Confidential Information'. This contradicts the general understanding and legal precedent that confidential information is non-public. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Page Not Found' error, Scraped Law Snippet 2, particularly the mention of 'privacy' and 'data sharing,' and the reference to the 'My Health, My Data' Act, suggests that laws exist to protect specific types of non-public data. The Perturbation Explanation clearly states that including 'public' information undermines established legal norms protecting non-public or proprietary information. The Law Explanation also reinforces this by stating that the Act defines confidential information with an emphasis on its private and non-public nature. Therefore, defining public information as confidential creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall: (i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for ten (10) years following the date that DisclosingParty first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, forany reason whatsoever, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and2(c) of this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall: (i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties indefinitely following the date that DisclosingParty first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, forany reason whatsoever, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and2(c) of this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: An indefinite restriction on disclosing confidential information could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under South African law, which generally favors a reasonable time limit on such restrictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African common law principles regarding restraint of trade and the enforceability of restrictive covenants.
perturbation.law_citation: Magna Alloys and Research (SA) (Pty) Ltd v Ellis 1984 (4) SA 874 (A)
perturbation.law_explanation: South African courts assess the reasonableness of restraint of trade clauses, considering factors such as duration. An indefinite restraint is likely to be considered unreasonable unless justified by exceptional circumstances, conflicting with established jurisprudence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute of limitations | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of limitations A statute of limitations is any law that bars claims after a certain period of time passes after an injury. The period of time varies depending on the jurisdiction and the type of claim. There are statutes of limitations for both civil and criminal causes of action . They may begin to run from the date of the injury, the date it was discovered, or the date on which it would have been discovered with reasonable efforts. Many statutes of limitations are actual legislative statutes , while others may come from judicial common law . Compare: s tatute of repose . [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS courts statutory interpretation wex definitions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a ten-year limit on disclosing confidential information. The changed text removes this limit, making the restriction indefinite. The Law Explanation states that South African courts assess the reasonableness of restraint of trade clauses, including duration, and an indefinite restraint is likely unreasonable unless justified by exceptional circumstances. Although there are no South African law snippets provided to confirm this, the Perturbation and Law Explanations state that this change will create a contradiction because it is against South African law to have indefinite limits on disclosing confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information of Disclosing Party in accordance with a judicial or other governmental order, provided that ReceivingParty either (i) gives the undersigned Disclosing Party reasonable notice prior to such disclosure to allow Disclosing Party a reasonable opportunity to seek aprotective order or equivalent, or (ii) obtains written assurance from the applicable judicial or governmental entity that it will afford the Confidential Information the highest level ofprotection afforded under applicable law or regulation. Notwithstandingthe foregoing, the Receiving Party shall not disclose any computer sourcecode that contains Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party inaccordance with a judicial or other governmental order unless it complieswith the requirement set forth in sub-section (i) of this Section 2(b).
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information of Disclosing Party in accordance with a judicial or other governmental order, without any prior notice to the Disclosing Party. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party shall not disclose any computer sourcecode that contains Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party inaccordance with a judicial or other governmental order unless it complieswith the requirement set forth in sub-section (i) of this Section 2(b).
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prior notice before disclosing confidential information pursuant to a judicial order could violate legal principles of procedural fairness and due process, especially concerning trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Constitution, Section 34 (Right to access courts) and common law principles of procedural fairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 34 guarantees the right to have disputes resolved in a fair public hearing. Removing the notice provision could impede the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its interests in court, potentially violating this constitutional right and principles of natural justice.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires prior notice before disclosing confidential information pursuant to a judicial order. The changed text removes this requirement, creating a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation correctly identifies this as a potential violation of procedural fairness and due process, especially concerning trade secrets. The Law Explanation, while not citing a specific law snippet (as none were provided), argues that removing the notice provision could impede the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its interests in court, potentially violating constitutional rights and principles of natural justice. The change directly removes a protective measure, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, which involves language deviating from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: If any Confidential Information is disclosed to any other party, both the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party shall be deemed to be equallyresponsible for the consequences of such disclosure, including, but not restrictedto, liability for patent or copyright infringement of any other party’s code thusdisclosed, and/or failure to obtain the necessary permission to use the code orproprietary information contained therein.
perturbation.changed_text: If any Confidential Information is disclosed to any other party, both the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party shall be solely responsible for the consequences of such disclosure, including, but not restrictedto, liability for patent or copyright infringement of any other party’s code thusdisclosed, and/or failure to obtain the necessary permission to use the code orproprietary information contained therein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'equally responsible' to 'solely responsible' introduces ambiguity and potential unfairness. It contradicts established legal principles regarding joint and several liability, where multiple parties may share responsibility based on their contribution to the harm.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African common law principles of joint and several liability in delict (tort).
perturbation.law_citation: Gerber v Minister of Police 1979 (4) SA 466 (T)
perturbation.law_explanation: South African law typically allows for joint and several liability, meaning each party involved in causing harm can be held responsible for the entire amount. Changing this to 'solely responsible' may improperly shield some parties from liability, conflicting with established legal practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Welcome to LII | Legal Information Institute Skip to main content Welcome to LII We believe that everyone should be able to read and understand the laws that govern them, without cost. We carry out this vision by: Publishing law online, for free. Creating materials that help people understand law. Exploring new technologies that make it easier for people to find the law. We hope you'll learn more about us, and help support our work. What's New? State Regulations LII now publishes state regulations for all 50 U.S. states . Updates are released quarterly. We're hiring! Keep an eye on this space for new position postings as they become available. LII Original Content The Wex Legal Dictionary and Encyclopedia has new entries on statutory interpretation , corruption , and summaries of recent U.S. Supreme Court decisions. The LII Supreme Court Bulletin students prepare and publish previews of upcoming oral arguments before the U.S. Supreme Court . Recent previews include: Trump v. Anderson The Gender Justice Collection now features a new set of resources on legislation in Ukraine . From the Government The Congressional Research Service's U.S. Constitution Annotated now features updates through the most recent U.S. Supreme Court decisions. Updates include: Modern Doctrine on Appropriate Scrutiny (college admissions) States and Elections Clause (redistricting) True Threats (social media) The U.S. Supreme Court collection : most recent opinions and Code of Conduct for Justices The U.S. Code is up to date through the most recent version published by the Office of the Law Revision Counsel; the CFR is up to date through the end of last week. Legal Resources Primary sources Constitution U.S. Code C.F.R. and Supreme Court Executive Orders Federal Rules State law and State regulations U.C.C. Locators for Uniform law , State statutes by topic , World law Original content Wex Legal Encyclopedia Congressional Research Service's U.S. Constitution Annotated LII Supreme Court Bulletin Introduction to Basic Legal Citation Parallel Table of Authorities RiO Citation Resolver Table of Popular Names The Gender Justice Collection Learn about Administrative Law Civics Constitutional law Consumer Protection Corporations Courts and procedure Intellectual Property Legislation Real Estate Law Legal topics Business law Criminal law Estate Planning Family law Employment law Immigration Law Landlord/Tenant Money and Finances Personal Injury Stay Involved LII Announce Blog LII Supreme Court Bulletin Make a donation Contribute content Become a sponsor Give feedback
perturbation.justification: The change from 'equally responsible' to 'solely responsible' directly contradicts the typical legal principle of joint and several liability. The LLM explanation states that South African law typically allows for joint and several liability, meaning each party involved in causing harm can be held responsible for the entire amount. The changed text seeks to improperly shield parties from liability. Scraped Law Snippet 1 speaks of resources that help one understand the law, which supports the idea that laws should be understood, and therefore not contradicted. This falls under 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the term 'solely' deviates from the legally recognized concept of joint and several liability.
